content.text: GitOps with Flux v2 on Arc Kubernetes

Flux v2 is the GitOps engine for Arc-enabled Kubernetes. Once the microsoft.flux extension is installed on a connected cluster, you create FluxConfiguration ARM resources that point to a git repository and define which paths to apply as Kustomizations or Helm releases. The Arc config-agent pushes the FluxConfiguration spec to the in-cluster Flux controllers. Flux's source-controller polls the git repository on the configured interval (default 10 minutes), the kustomize-controller applies changed manifests, and the helm-controller reconciles Helm releases. The key principle: Flux only pulls from git — it never pushes from Azure to the cluster directly. All cluster state changes are driven by commits to the repository.

attrs.alt: GitOps with Flux v2: developer git push → FluxConfiguration ARM resource → config-agent → Flux controllers reconcile cluster state from git

content.text: Critical: Set prune=true on every kustomization unless you explicitly want orphaned resources to persist after removal from git. The default is false, which means deleting a manifest from git does not delete the K8s object — this is the most common source of drift in Flux deployments.
Git repository HTTPS or SSH URL
How often Flux polls the repo
Max time for a sync operation
Delete K8s objects removed from repo
Setting Up Flux on Arc Clusters

Install the Flux extension first, then create FluxConfiguration resources. Each FluxConfiguration can have multiple kustomization entries — one for infrastructure (Gatekeeper, cert-manager, external-secrets) and one for applications is a common pattern. The sync_interval controls how often Flux polls the repo; set it to 2-5 minutes for active development environments. Use 'flux reconcile' from the Flux CLI or 'az k8s-configuration flux show' from the Az CLI to check sync state and trigger immediate reconciliation.

# Flux v2 GitOps: install extension and create FluxConfiguration

# 1. Install Flux extension on connected cluster
az k8s-extension create \
  --name gitops \
  --extension-type microsoft.flux \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --scope cluster

# Verify Flux CRDs and controllers are running
kubectl get pods -n flux-system
kubectl get crd | grep flux

# 2. Create FluxConfiguration for app deployment from GitHub
az k8s-configuration flux create \
  --name 'app-config' \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --namespace flux-system \
  --scope cluster \
  --url 'https://github.com/<org>/fleet-gitops' \
  --branch main \
  --kustomization name=apps path=./apps/prod prune=true sync_interval=5m retry_interval=30s timeout=5m

# 3. Show FluxConfiguration sync status
az k8s-configuration flux show \
  --name 'app-config' \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query '{Status:complianceState, Synced:statuses[0].appliedAt, Conditions:statuses[0].conditions}' \
  -o json

# 4. Force immediate sync (without waiting for interval)
# Install Flux CLI (fluxcd.io/install)
flux reconcile source git app-config -n flux-system
flux reconcile kustomization apps -n flux-system

# 5. Check Flux status via kubectl
kubectl get gitrepositories -n flux-system
kubectl get kustomizations -n flux-system
flux get all -n flux-system

# 6. Update FluxConfiguration (change branch or add kustomization path)
az k8s-configuration flux update \
  --name 'app-config' \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --branch 'release/v2' \
  --kustomization name=apps path=./apps/prod-v2 prune=true sync_interval=10m

For a fleet of Arc-connected clusters, the standard pattern is a single git repository with a base kustomization layer (shared across all clusters) and environment-specific overlays per cluster. Each cluster's FluxConfiguration points to its own overlay path. Use a loop to apply the same FluxConfiguration to all clusters in the fleet. Enforce GitOps on new clusters via Azure Policy DINE — the built-in policy 'Configure Flux extension on Arc K8s' deploys the Flux extension and creates a default FluxConfiguration when a new cluster is Arc-connected.

# Multi-cluster GitOps: same config applied to multiple Arc K8s clusters
# Pattern: base kustomization shared; environment-specific overlays per cluster

# Git repo structure for multi-cluster fleet:
# fleet-gitops/
#   base/
#     deployment.yaml
#     service.yaml
#     kustomization.yaml
#   overlays/
#     prod-east/    (for edge-cluster-prod-eastus)
#       kustomization.yaml (extends base, sets replicas=3)
#     prod-west/    (for edge-cluster-prod-westus)
#       kustomization.yaml (extends base, sets replicas=2)
#     staging/
#       kustomization.yaml (extends base, sets replicas=1, image tag=latest)

# Apply FluxConfiguration to each cluster pointing to its own overlay path:
CLUSTERS=(edge-cluster-prod-eastus edge-cluster-prod-westus edge-cluster-staging)
PATHS=(./overlays/prod-east ./overlays/prod-west ./overlays/staging)
RG='arc-k8s-rg'

for i in "${!CLUSTERS[@]}"; do
  cluster="${CLUSTERS[$i]}"
  path="${PATHS[$i]}"
  echo "Configuring GitOps for $cluster ($path)"
  az k8s-configuration flux create \
    --name 'fleet-app-config' \
    --cluster-type connectedClusters \
    --cluster-name "$cluster" \
    --resource-group "$RG" \
    --namespace flux-system \
    --scope cluster \
    --url 'https://github.com/<org>/fleet-gitops' \
    --branch main \
    --kustomization name=apps path="$path" prune=true sync_interval=5m
done

# List all FluxConfigurations across the fleet
az k8s-configuration flux list \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod-eastus' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --query '[].{Name:name, Status:complianceState, Branch:sourceKind}' \
  -o table

# Azure Policy: enforce GitOps on all Arc K8s clusters
# Built-in: 'Configure Flux extension on Arc K8s'
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'require-gitops-arc-k8s' \
  --display-name 'Require Flux GitOps on all Arc K8s clusters' \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/b7ef244e-1b6d-4b58-a60a-1b3e3ac3a3e7' \
  --params '{
    "configurationName":{"value":"fleet-app-config"},
    "sourceControlUrl":{"value":"https://github.com/<org>/fleet-gitops"},
    "branch":{"value":"main"},
    "kustomizationPath":{"value":"./base"}
  }' \
  --scope '/subscriptions/<sub>' \
  --mi-system-assigned \
  --location eastus

For private repositories, FluxConfiguration supports HTTPS with a personal access token (PAT) and SSH with a deploy key. GitHub PATs can be passed as --https-key at FluxConfiguration creation — the Arc control plane stores the credential encrypted in the ARM resource. For SSH, generate an Ed25519 key pair, add the public key as a read-only Deploy Key in the repository, and pass the private key as --ssh-private-key. Azure DevOps uses HTTPS with a PAT (the username field is the ADO organization or service account). Store long-lived credentials in Key Vault and rotate them on a schedule.

# GitOps with private repositories: SSH key and token authentication

# Option 1: HTTPS with GitHub Personal Access Token (PAT)
#           Store in Kubernetes secret; referenced in FluxConfiguration
kubectl create secret generic github-pat \
  --namespace flux-system \
  --from-literal=username='git' \
  --from-literal=password='<github-pat>'

az k8s-configuration flux create \
  --name 'private-config' \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --namespace flux-system \
  --scope cluster \
  --url 'https://github.com/<org>/private-fleet-gitops' \
  --branch main \
  --https-user git \
  --https-key '<github-pat>' \
  --kustomization name=apps path=./apps/prod prune=true

# Option 2: SSH key authentication
# Generate SSH key pair for the FluxConfiguration
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C 'flux-arc@edge-cluster-prod' -f ./flux_deploy_key -N ''

# Add the public key as a Deploy Key in GitHub (read-only access to the repo)
cat ./flux_deploy_key.pub
# GitHub: Settings → Deploy Keys → Add deploy key (read access only)

az k8s-configuration flux create \
  --name 'private-ssh-config' \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --namespace flux-system \
  --scope cluster \
  --url 'git@github.com:<org>/private-fleet-gitops.git' \
  --branch main \
  --ssh-private-key "$(cat ./flux_deploy_key | base64)" \
  --ssh-private-key-file ./flux_deploy_key \
  --kustomization name=apps path=./apps/prod prune=true

# Option 3: Azure DevOps with service account PAT
az k8s-configuration flux create \
  --name 'ado-config' \
  --cluster-type connectedClusters \
  --cluster-name 'edge-cluster-prod' \
  --resource-group 'arc-k8s-rg' \
  --namespace flux-system \
  --scope cluster \
  --url 'https://dev.azure.com/<org>/<project>/_git/<repo>' \
  --branch main \
  --https-user '<ado-pat-username>' \
  --https-key '<ado-pat-token>' \
  --kustomization name=infra path=./infrastructure prune=true

# Verify authentication works
kubectl get gitrepository private-config -n flux-system -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*]}'
# Look for: 'type: Ready, status: True'
# If: 'authentication failed' — re-check PAT permissions or SSH key is added as deploy key
Deleted manifests from git leave orphaned K8s resources
Always set prune=true for production Flux kustomizations
Using same FluxConfiguration path for all clusters
All clusters get identical config; no environment differentiation
Use Kustomize overlays per cluster or environment; point each cluster to its overlay path
Credentials visible to anyone with repo access
Use Sealed Secrets, SOPS encryption, or External Secrets Operator to store secrets outside git
Not setting sync_interval short enough for CI/CD
Deployments after git push take up to 10 min to appear (default interval)
Set sync_interval=2m or use Flux webhook receiver for immediate reconciliation
Forgetting to add SSH public key as Deploy Key in repo
Add the Flux-generated public key to GitHub/ADO as a deploy key with read access
Using FluxConfiguration scope=namespace for cluster-wide resources
Cluster-scoped resources (CRDs, ClusterRoles) fail to apply
Use scope=cluster for configurations that include cluster-scoped manifests

Flux's helm-controller manages Helm chart deployments via HelmRelease custom resources. A HelmRelease references a HelmRepository (the chart source — HTTPS or OCI) and declares values overrides, upgrade retry policy, and rollback configuration. For Azure Container Registry (ACR), use OCI-type HelmRepository with a docker-registry secret for authentication. ImageUpdateAutomation automatically creates pull requests when a new container image tag is pushed to the registry — Flux detects the new tag, updates the image reference in the git repo, and opens a PR. This closes the CI/CD loop without requiring pipeline integration.

# Flux HelmRelease: deploy Helm charts to Arc K8s clusters from git or OCI registry

# Git repo structure for HelmRelease manifests:
# fleet-gitops/
#   infrastructure/
#     cert-manager/
#       helmrelease.yaml
#       helmrepo.yaml
#     nginx-ingress/
#       helmrelease.yaml
#       helmrepo.yaml
#   apps/
#     my-app/
#       helmrelease.yaml

# Example HelmRelease manifest (apps/my-app/helmrelease.yaml)
cat << 'EOF' > helmrelease.yaml
apiVersion: helm.toolkit.fluxcd.io/v2beta1
kind: HelmRelease
metadata:
  name: my-app
  namespace: default
spec:
  interval: 10m
  chart:
    spec:
      chart: my-app
      version: '>=1.0.0 <2.0.0'
      sourceRef:
        kind: HelmRepository
        name: my-app-repo
        namespace: flux-system
  values:
    replicaCount: 3
    image:
      repository: myregistry.azurecr.io/my-app
      tag: latest
  upgrade:
    remediation:
      retries: 3
  rollback:
    timeout: 5m
EOF

# HelmRepository manifest (points Flux to the chart repository)
cat << 'EOF' > helmrepo.yaml
apiVersion: source.toolkit.fluxcd.io/v1beta2
kind: HelmRepository
metadata:
  name: my-app-repo
  namespace: flux-system
spec:
  interval: 5m
  url: https://charts.myorg.com
  # For OCI (Azure Container Registry):
  # type: oci
  # url: oci://myregistry.azurecr.io/charts
EOF

# ACR authentication for OCI Helm charts: create secret in flux-system namespace
kubectl create secret docker-registry acr-creds \
  --namespace flux-system \
  --docker-server 'myregistry.azurecr.io' \
  --docker-username '<sp-client-id>' \
  --docker-password '<sp-client-secret>'

# Add secretRef to HelmRepository for private ACR
cat << 'EOF' >> helmrepo.yaml
  secretRef:
    name: acr-creds
EOF

# Force immediate HelmRelease reconciliation
flux reconcile helmrelease my-app -n default

# Check HelmRelease status (Ready / Progressing / Failed)
kubectl get helmreleases -n default
flux get helmrelease -n default

# Rollback a failed HelmRelease
flux suspend helmrelease my-app -n default
flux resume helmrelease my-app -n default
# Or manual rollback:
helm rollback my-app 1 -n default

# ImageUpdateAutomation: auto-create PRs when new image tag is pushed
cat << 'EOF' > imageupdateautomation.yaml
apiVersion: image.toolkit.fluxcd.io/v1beta1
kind: ImageUpdateAutomation
metadata:
  name: my-app-update
  namespace: flux-system
spec:
  interval: 5m
  sourceRef:
    kind: GitRepository
    name: fleet-gitops
  git:
    checkout:
      ref:
        branch: main
    commit:
      author:
        email: flux@myorg.com
        name: Flux
      messageTemplate: 'auto: update my-app to {{ .Updated.Images }}'
    push:
      branch: auto-image-update
  update:
    path: ./apps/my-app
    strategy: Setters
EOF

Container Insights (azure-monitor extension on Arc K8s) ships Kubernetes events and pod inventory to Log Analytics. Key signals for GitOps health: Flux Warning events indicate reconciliation failures; pods in Flux-managed namespaces without Flux ownership annotations (app.kubernetes.io/managed-by) indicate resources created outside GitOps (drift); high restart counts signal bad image tags or broken configuration applied by Flux; flux-system pod health monitors Flux controller availability. Set alert rules on Flux controller log messages to catch sync failures within minutes.

Flux v2 GitOps on Arc-enabled Kubernetes uses FluxConfiguration ARM resources to continuously reconcile cluster state to a git repository. Install the microsoft.flux extension first, then create FluxConfiguration with url, branch, and kustomization paths. Always set prune=true to ensure removed manifests are deleted. Use Kustomize overlays for multi-cluster fleet deployments — one overlay per cluster, one FluxConfiguration per cluster pointing to its path. Use HTTPS PAT or SSH deploy key for private repositories. Enforce GitOps on new clusters via Azure Policy DINE. The config-agent on the cluster bridges the ARM FluxConfiguration to the in-cluster Flux controllers — Flux always pulls from git, never receives pushes.
